item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By any chance, was this inspired by D. B. Cooper?

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Correct me if I’m wrong, but shouldn’t it be “observed?”

Thanks for bringing that to our attention! The fix has been made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh dear, I’m quite late. I do think there is a typo in the 28-33 seconds behavior entry.

“…and is observing writhing on the ground, screaming in apparent agony.”

Correct me if I’m wrong, but shouldn’t it be “observed?”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this, so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The guy is "walking" at eight miles an hour? More like jogging, or running.

Also think if he's close enough to get blasted like that, he would've been close enough to be blinded by the initial flash.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We're all fond of replying to million year-old posts.
ALL of us.

item.title: Proposed changes
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to propose some changes to SCP-1033 to make it a lot more scientifically accurate.

Set of changes, top to bottom:

"lined with 5cm thick lead plating." changed to "lined with 5m thick lead plating."
Reason: 5 centimeters won't do anything in regards to the dose rates, see below

"Foundation researchers found that SCP-1033 was emitting well over the lethal limit of radiation, approximately 15,000 mSv." changed to

"Foundation researchers found that SCP-1033 was emitting well over the instantaneous lethal dose rate limit of high-energy gamma, neutron, and x radiation at approximately 15,000Sv/hr."

Reason: 1) You can't emit a dose instantaneously. If you do, you're breaking quantum physics and this jumps from a Euclid to something that has already ended the world.
2) 15kSv/hr is enough to kill any living human being nearly instantly, due to the sheer dose rate, not necessarily absorbed dose.
3) Gamma, neutrons, and x-rays are all emitted during a nuclear blast, which nicely links to the bottom. This is a lot more descriptive than "radiation". Not exactly necessary, as sieverts would be enough, but this is a lot better scientifically.

"1.5 megaton nuclear device being detonated." changed to "1.5 megaton nuclear device being detonated in close proximity."

Reason: detonating a 1.5 megaton nuke won't do anything to you if you're on the other side of the planet. Clarity.

Source: I do nuclear stuff, I'm qualified to talk about it :)

Edit: DOH, I replied to a comment instead of making a new post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This I like. Now enjoy your up vote +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why the specifics about the guns? Why not just "Heavy machine guns" or something

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a very interesting concept but… I don't know, it's lacking something. I feel there should be juuuust a little bit more about it so we can speculate more on his story. Reading other comments, I also realized I somehow missed the implication of the phone number not existing yet… except I feel it actually makes the SCP less threatening: have the SCP Foundation forbid that number from being asigned and there you go, problem solved. Come to think of it, couldn't they grab one of the guy's hair or take a blood sample and identify him through DNA analysis?

I'm also rather confused as to how reasearchers came to the conclusion that "The injuries SCP-1033 sustains correspond with that of a victim of a 1.5 megaton nuclear device being detonated". That seems strangely specific, especially considering such devices have a rather wide area of effect, presumably affecting victims in very different ways depending on how close to the epicentre they are. For the sake of reference, the bomb dropped on Hiroshima released 15 kilotons of energy, that's one-hundredth of the energy released by the device affecting SCP-1033.

All in all, not great but has potential. Neutral vote for now, alhough it seems unlikely that the original author will go back to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was missing the "Item #". I couldn't let this slip, as this is one of my favourite articles, so I added it per te.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image in this article was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that escalated quickly.

In any case, nice work in general. My only complaint is that the subject of this article is quite repetitive.

Get it? … Repetitive?… I'll shut up now.
God. Finally. 'bout time one of my favorite articles gets to +100.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still want to see a tale of this.

Also, this is one of my favorite SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think theyre should be something about what happens when they force him to stop, does he just phase through things? what if they steal the phone before he sends it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man is stripped of all his flesh? Man has unusual temporal qualities? Man is extremely radioactive? Perhaps you ought to call him Jon.

Not a problem at all, it takes a different enough direction. I enjoyed it immensely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read the article, then came to comment on these very issues. They have not been fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this not only because of the concept, but because of the implications. What happens if a stable time loops manages to diverge from itself? Moreso, if this is an entity from the future, when will it catch up with itself?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i just imagine some kid buying a cell phone with that exact number at a store one day, and the foundation just runs into the store, kidnaps the kid, and interrogates him.

Dr. J: WHO ARE YOU AND WHO DO YOU WORK FOR.

Bobby: uh… uh…

Dr. J: ANSWER ME DAMN IT WE'RE TRYING TO AVOID A NUCLEAR EXPLOSION.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the meaning behind this one and I just adore the possibility of what this thing could do in the future.

Another scary thing is the fact that most roads in Poland look like that one in the picture. Guess this one explains why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That flash with static… it really seemed to go with the flavor of time-travel, as I recalled the Terminator's entrance in one of the movies.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dazzling white flash is better than "vision encompassing".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking they would use the amount of time between when he starts to burn and when he's knocked back. The light moves at light speed, but the shockwave only moves at the speed of sound.

Also, they should mention the time between these.

I think they might be able to tell the size of the nuke based on the temperature, which they could find by looking at the light spectrum. Also, they might be able to tell based on how long it lasts. I'm not remotely an expert on this, so if anyone else knows, I'd appriciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does the Australian telephone system allot their numbers in numerical order? If so, then you could theoretically predict when this number would come up, and get an idea about the earliest this event would occur.

In addition, is the model of prepaid cell SCP-1033 is using on the market at this time? Is his briefcase presently being manufactured? These could help pinpoint the time of the event a little better.

Overall, a very interesting piece. I think it would be great if, as stated above, many of the temporal loops were all related to a single event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, personally, would love it if it turned out that all the temporal loops were caused/activated by the same thing. Like the machine in 176 not only fucked up the time-stream in that building, but caused time "bubbles" to attach to different people (this'un) and things (the Chicago Loop window), too.

item.title: Grenades
item.content:encoded:
One nitpick about the grenades issued to the guards: flash-bangs (or noise grenades), and concussive grenades are two separate devices.

The flash-bang has a perforated shell to minimize overpressure and allow the explosion to generate a lot of noise and light, but otherwise be harmless (burning aside).
A concussion grenade, on the other hand, is because it kills/incapacitates with the explosive force alone, not with shrapnel, by generating a massive overpressure with a larger-than-average explosive filler and a body made of tarred cardboard (in case of the US MK3A2) that doesn't contain the blast wave. It's not called a concussion grenade because it gives you a concussion, but because it kills you with the blunt force trauma. Setting one off in the hallway of a Foundation facility would kill every guard in the hallway from one end to the other…

That aside, I'd like to see if the Foundation can force the carrier in question not to allocate that number. It could determine whether our universe is a Novikov Type-I or Type-II, that is, if the future can be changed using foreknowledge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can keep the megatonnage as long as you also specify a distance from the blast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can know the distance by observing and timing various actions of the subject.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We've got quite a few time loop SCPs, but each of them is pretty different in their own way, and in this case too good to get rid of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's probably no need to mention megatons at all, just describe some effects suggestive of exposure to a nuclear blast. E.g. the subject's clothing begins to smoulder and bursts into flames, and a few seconds later he is swept away as though by a powerful blast wave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um… SCP-176, anyone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, cheers for all the great constructive criticism. Seeing as this is the first SCP I've written, it's great to see members of the community giving me a hand.

Secondly, the proposed changes I've got lined up are:
1. Extending the cycle to a minute.
2. Fixing up the scientific side of this SCP.
3. Getting a different title on SCP Series page to account for the cycle change.

Any other suggestions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woah, a wealth of information. I'll make some changes, thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Righto, thanks for those tips. I'm not exactly science savvy so this helps a great deal. ANy suggestions as to what I can use instead of megatons?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll probably do that when I get off work. I was writing that on a train when I was bored as all hell, so it's a bit off reality :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm with Yoric on this. They wouldn't overlook something like that, especially given the implications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good example of how good concept can overshadow sloppy execution.

The door to SCP-1033's cell is to also be made of 5cm of lead plating, with 10 cm steel alloy, with a three bolt redundancy lock, to only be opened by personnel with authorisation signed by staff with Level 4 clearance or higher only.

"Only" used twice, and also weird sentence structure.

accompanied by hearing a two-second burst of static from all directions. When questioned on the source of this static, one witness stated that "It was just everywhere man".

Italicized part is redundant.

The Foundation was alerted to SCP-1033 by Agent F██████, who was stationed within the NYPD at the time.

Don't really add anything. In some articles it's important to know how the Foundation found the object, but not in this one.

To contain SCP-1033, field researchers placed a shipping container in his way, sealing it and moving it to Storage Site K█, where he currently resides.

Tone a bit off. "SCP-1033 was contained by placing a shipping container in his way. The container was subsequently moved to …" may work better, but it's not perfect too.

is a normal, albeit balding, middle aged male.

Balding is not a deviation from normality. Also, he isn't really normal. Try something like "is an unremarkable, balding, middle aged male" or even "is a balding middle aged male".

Every 33.445 seconds, SCP-1033 begins his cycle.

"SCP-1033's behavior is cyclic, with each cycle taking 33.445 seconds". Also, you can just say "approximately 33 seconds". And I agree with RhettSarlin about extending the cycle to a minute or so.

You may want to go to chat and ask for help with tone. I have been known to suffer from tone issues myself, so my suggestions may be not perfect too. People in chat is usually helpful with thing like that, and having many people to proofread your writing is always better than one. And if you want to change a name, you may I ask about it there too.

But, yeah, good concept, I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Part of the containment procedures needs to be the Foundation registering and maintaining control of that phone number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote from me for now, for precisely these reasons. Fix 'em, and you'll have earned my upvote. The abovementioned temporal fuckery, as well as the general (and well-executed) lack of explanation, makes this for me otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is okay, but a couple of things in the description don't ring true scientifically.

"SCP-1033 was emitting well over the lethal limit of radiation, approximately 15,000 mSv"

Sieverts are a measure of the total radiation dose received by a subject over a period of time, whereas this sentence seems to be talking about the rate of radiation emission by the source itself. That would be measured in curies or becquerels. Also it makes no sense to talk about whether a source of radiation is lethal or not, as that depends entirely on distance and length of exposure.

"The injuries SCP-1033 sustains correspond with that of a victim of a 1.5 megaton nuclear device being detonated."

This makes no sense either, as the effect would depend tremendously on how far away the subject was from the centre of the blast, and there doesn't seem to be a way of estimating that from the information available.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason, I feel like he should say 'Are we cool yet?" instead of chuckling. Other then that, awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You cannot know the megatonage of a device from the radiation unless you know the distance.

Okay, you can estimate it based on the time from when he sends the message to when he starts to lose bits of flesh… Assuming he's the one that sets it off!

Personally, I'd put a D-Class (in a rad suit) next to the guy and steal his phone and the envelope…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could use the word "Noise" instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interviews with witnesses found that people in the area experienced a vision-encompassing white flash, accompanied by hearing a two-second burst of static from all directions. When questioned on the source of this static, one witness stated that "it was just everywhere man".

Y/N?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I advise adding a few seconds to each stage here. 30 paces in 10 seconds isn't really walking….typing and sending that message in 6 seconds is really fast. You could basically extend his cycle to a full minute without it feeling lengthy.

That said, I love this. Upvoted.

*edit* try it….try walking at a pace of 3 steps every second, checking your wrist every 10 steps….i just did, that was crazy :P

basically i suggest taking everything that you're having him do and go through the motions, recording yourself doing it. time yourself, and find out how long it really should take for each section, and then use that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interviews with witnesses found that people in the area experienced a vision-encompassing white flash, accompanied by hearing a two-second burst of static from all directions.

Maybe something like that? I'm not too sure myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You are right on that, I'll trim that off.

In regards to the static, the intended meaning was that the witnesses were unsure of where the static came from, as if it just played inside their heads. Any tips on how to convey this while maintaining a professional tone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple things:

If SCP-1033 attempts an escape, all efforts are to be made to stop it to prevent massive radiation contamination.

This seems superfluous, as of course the Foundation is going to try and keep him from escaping, regardless of the reason why.

Interviews with witnesses found that people in the area experienced a vision-encompassing white flash, accompanied by a two-second burst of static from an unknown source.

I'm a little unclear on this. Are they hearing a noise like static from a TV or radio just in the air? Or are radios in the area suddenly playing static? It could maybe use some clarification.

Other than that, I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed the 35, that was my bad.

He doesn't move, he just reappears at the start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's exactly what I was going for, glad you love it!

Also, fixing up all the edits you guys have pointed out, thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is very good, but in general, crosslinking SCPs is not a good idea. The radiation dose might be a bit low, also. The lethal dose of radiation at the Goiaina disaster in 1987 was 4000-6000 mSv, and people survived doses of 7000 mSv.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea. I'm upvoting based on the idea. Parts of the execution are a little weird, though. The containment procedures are really specific, maybe more so than is necessary. Your next-to-last line has a spot of "redacting multiple sentences to change tone suddenly", a thing I hate (though you wouldn't be the only person here doing that). And the last sentence, the crosslink, is a wee unnecessary.

But upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What does he do from 21 seconds to 28 seconds?

EDIT: Oh man, I forgot to tell you why I love this.

It stems from that phone number not being used yet. Combined with the fact that there's already temporal fuckery going on, it's reasonable to suppose that this loop started in the future. Which means that, at some point, he's probably going to cause an actual nuclear explosion. At a Foundation site.

Does that make sense at all, or am I missing some obvious thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not… bad.

Does he move between blips? How do we know he is 35?
